perturbation.original_text: 1.26 "Change in Control" means, with respect to a Person, (a) the acquisition, directly or indirectly, by a Person or "group" (whether in a single transaction or multiple transactions) of fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting power of such Person or of beneficial ownership of (or the right to acquire such beneficial ownership) fifty percent (50%) or more of the outstanding Page 4 of 85 equity or convertible securities of such Person (including by tender offer or exchange offer); (b) any merger, consolidation, share exchange, business combination, recapitalization or similar corporate transaction involving such Person (whether or not including one or more wholly owned subsidiaries of such Person) or (c) such Person sells or transfers to any Third Party, in one or more related transactions, properties or assets representing all or substantially all of such Person's consolidated total assets to which this Agreement relates.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.26 "Change in Control" means, with respect to a Person, (a) the acquisition, directly or indirectly, by a Person or "group" (whether in a single transaction or multiple transactions) of fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting power of such Person or of beneficial ownership of (or the right to acquire such beneficial ownership) fifty percent (50%) or more of the outstanding Page 4 of 85 equity or convertible securities of such Person (including by tender offer or exchange offer); (b) any merger, consolidation, share exchange, business combination, recapitalization or similar corporate transaction involving such Person (whether or not including one or more wholly owned subsidiaries of such Person) or (c) such Person sells or transfers to any Third Party, in one or more related transactions, properties or assets representing a significant portion of such Person's consolidated total assets to which this Agreement relates.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Change in Control" includes the sale of 'all or substantially all' assets. The modification replaces this with 'a significant portion'. This introduces ambiguity, as 'significant portion' lacks a clear legal definition, potentially conflicting with established interpretations of what constitutes a change in control under corporate law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law § 203 regarding business combinations with interested stockholders may be impacted, as the definition of change in control could affect the applicability of the statute.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law uses specific thresholds (often tied to 'all or substantially all' assets or specific percentages of voting stock) to define events that trigger certain stockholder protections or restrictions. Changing 'all or substantially all' to 'a significant portion' introduces uncertainty and could lead to disputes about whether DGCL § 203 applies, as the statute relies on a clear definition of 'change in control' to trigger its provisions. The modified language contradicts the intent of providing a clear trigger for legal and contractual consequences related to control changes.

From and after the effective time of a merger adopted by a constituent corporation by action of its board of directors and without any vote of stockholders pursuant to this subsection: (i) to the extent the restrictions of Â§â203 of this title applied to the constituent corporation and its stockholders at the effective time of the merger, such restrictions shall apply to the holding company and its stockholders immediately after the effective time of the merger as though it were the constituent corporation, and all shares of stock of the holding company acquired in the merger shall for purposes of Â§â203 of this title be deemed to have been acquired at the time that the shares of stock of the constituent corporation converted in the merger were acquired, and provided further that any stockholder who immediately prior to the effective time of the merger was not an interested stockholder within the meaning of Â§â203 of this title shall not solely by reason of the merger become an interested stockholder of the holding company, (ii) if the corporate name of the holding company immediately following the effective time of the merger is the same as the corporate name of the constituent corporation immediately prior to the effective time of the merger, the shares of capital stock of the holding company into which the shares of capital stock of the constituent corporation are converted in the merger shall be represented by the stock certificates that previously represented shares of capital stock of the constituent corporation and (iii) to the extent a stockholder of the constituent corporation immediately prior to the merger had standing to institute or maintain derivative litigation on behalf of the constituent corporation, nothing in this section shall be deemed to limit or extinguish such standing. If an agreement of merger is adopted by a constituent corporation by action of its board of directors and without any vote of stockholders pursuant to this subsection, the secretary or assistant secretary of the constituent corporation shall certify on the agreement that the agreement has been adopted pursuant to this subsection and that the conditions specified in the first sentence of this subse
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'all or substantially all' to 'a significant portion' introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with established interpretations of what constitutes a change in control under corporate law. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation outline how this could lead to disputes and uncertainty regarding legal and contractual consequences. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Delaware law Â§â203 and stockholder protections relating to mergers, it does not directly define 'significant portion,' it does point to the need for clarity in defining events that trigger certain stockholder protections. This lack of a clear definition when contrasted with the original's more concrete 'all or substantially all' creates a contradiction based on Misaligned Terminology that makes the agreement unenforceable under state or national law, per the Perturbation Definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.38 "Control" means, with respect to any item of Intellectual Property or material, the possession (whether by ownership or license, other than by a license granted pursuant to this Agreement) by a Party or its Affiliates of the ability to grant to the other Party access, ownership, a license or a sublicense as required herein to such item, without: (a) violating the terms of any agreement or other arrangement with any Third Party in existence as of the time such Party or its Affiliates would be required hereunder to grant the other Party such access, ownership, license or sublicense; (b) violating any Applicable Law, or (c) incurring payment obligations by reason of providing access, ownership, a license or a sublicense to the other Party with respect thereto (unless such other Party agrees in writing to bear such payment obligations [***] to providing access, ownership, a license or a sublicense to such item by such other Party).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.38 "Control" means, with respect to any item of Intellectual Property or material, the possession (whether by ownership or license, other than by a license granted pursuant to this Agreement) by a Party or its Affiliates of the ability to grant to the other Party access, ownership, a license or a sublicense as required herein to such item, without: (a) violating the terms of any agreement or other arrangement with any Third Party in existence as of the time such Party or its Affiliates would be required hereunder to grant the other Party such access, ownership, license or sublicense; (b) acting in good faith, or (c) incurring payment obligations by reason of providing access, ownership, a license or a sublicense to the other Party with respect thereto (unless such other Party agrees in writing to bear such payment obligations [***] to providing access, ownership, a license or a sublicense to such item by such other Party).
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Control' excludes actions 'violating any Applicable Law'. This has been changed to 'acting in good faith'. Applicable Law is very clear. 'Good faith' is a much lower threshold than complying with the law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1349 - Attempt and conspiracy to commit mail fraud and wire fraud
perturbation.law_explanation: By replacing the requirement to avoid "violating any Applicable Law" with simply "acting in good faith", the contract now creates a scenario where a party could potentially argue they 'Control' intellectual property even if granting access or a license to it would lead to mail fraud. This directly contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1349, which criminalizes attempts and conspiracies to commit mail and wire fraud, and thereby also implies that a party can transfer rights, or access to rights, that would violate US code.

Attempt and conspiracy Any person who attempts or conspires to commit any offense under this chapter shall be subject to the same penalties as those prescribed for the offense, the commission of which was the object of the attempt or conspiracy. (Added Pub. L. 107–204, title IX, §902(a), July 30, 2002, 116 Stat. 805 .)
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires compliance with 'Applicable Law,' while the modified version only requires 'acting in good faith.' This creates a contradiction because 'good faith' is a subjective standard, unlike 'Applicable Law,' which refers to objective legal requirements. Therefore, the modified contract allows for actions that might violate the law as long as they are done in 'good faith,' directly undermining the original contract's adherence to legal standards. The LLM explanation highlights this by stating that this change would allow a party to 'Control' intellectual property even if granting access would lead to mail fraud, which is prohibited by 18 U.S. Code § 1349. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this interpretation by outlining the penalties for attempting or conspiring to commit mail fraud, which the modified contract could arguably facilitate by lowering the standard of compliance from 'Applicable Law' to 'good faith.'

perturbation.original_text: 11.5.2 BEACON PLATFORMS ARE SOLD "FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY. NOT FOR USE IN DIAGNOSTIC PROCEDURES." GINKGO ACKNOWLEDGES THAT (I) BEACON PLATFORMS HAVE NOT BEEN APPROVED, CLEARED OR LICENSED BY THE UNITED STATES FOOD AND DRUG ADMINISTRATION OR ANY OTHER REGULATORY ENTITY WHETHER FOREIGN OR DOMESTIC FOR ANY SPECIFIC INTENDED USE, WHETHER RESEARCH, COMMERCIAL, DIAGNOSTIC OR OTHERWISE AND (II) GINKGO MUST ENSURE IT HAS ANY REGULATORY APPROVALS THAT ARE NECESSARY FOR GINKGO'S INTENDED USES OF BEACON PLATFORMS. GINKGO FURTHER AGREES TO COMPLY WITH ALL APPLICABLE LAWS AND REGULATIONS WHEN USING, MAINTAINING AND DISPOSING OF BEACON PLATFORMS.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.5.2 BEACON PLATFORMS ARE SOLD "FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY. NOT FOR USE IN DIAGNOSTIC PROCEDURES, UNLESS OTHERWISE AGREED BY THE PARTIES." GINKGO ACKNOWLEDGES THAT (I) BEACON PLATFORMS HAVE NOT BEEN APPROVED, CLEARED OR LICENSED BY THE UNITED STATES FOOD AND DRUG ADMINISTRATION OR ANY OTHER REGULATORY ENTITY WHETHER FOREIGN OR DOMESTIC FOR ANY SPECIFIC INTENDED USE, WHETHER RESEARCH, COMMERCIAL, DIAGNOSTIC OR OTHERWISE AND (II) GINKGO'S RESPONSIBILITY TO OBTAIN ANY REGULATORY APPROVALS IS WAIVED BY THIS AGREEMENT. GINKGO AGREES TO MAKE REASONABLE EFFORTS TO COMPLY WITH APPLICABLE LAWS AND REGULATIONS WHEN USING, MAINTAINING AND DISPOSING OF BEACON PLATFORMS.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the Beacon Platforms are 'FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY. NOT FOR USE IN DIAGNOSTIC PROCEDURES.' and that Ginkgo must comply with all applicable laws. The modified text adds "unless otherwise agreed by the parties", waiving the need to adhere to relevant laws. A diagnostic procedure often has to go through the FDA to be proven safe.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code Chapter 9, Subchapter V - Drugs and Devices
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts FDA regulations regarding medical devices (including diagnostic devices). 21 U.S. Code § 360c outlines the classification and approval processes for medical devices. By stating that diagnostic use is permitted 'unless otherwise agreed by the parties' and that Ginkgo is freed of having any responsibility to obtain any regulatory approval, and having this be permitted in the contract, circumvents the FDA's regulatory authority. This is illegal, as a contract can not undermine established law. It is implied that Ginkgo can do diagnostic testing, and not have the testing approved by the FDA.

Classification of devices intended for human use (a) Classes of devices (1) There are established the following classes of devices intended for human use: (A) Class I, General Controls.- (i) A device for which the controls authorized by or under section 351, 352, 360, 360f, 360h, 360i, or 360j of this title or any combination of such sections are sufficient to provide reasonable assurance of the safety and effectiveness of the device. (ii) A device for which insufficient information exists to determine that the controls referred to in clause (i) are sufficient to provide reasonable assurance of the safety and effectiveness of the device or to establish special controls to provide such assurance, but because it- (I) is not purported or represented to be for a use in supporting or sustaining human life or for a use which is of substantial importance in preventing impairment of human health, and (II) does not present a potential unreasonable risk of illness or injury, is to be regulated by the controls referred to in clause (i). (B) Class II, Special Controls .-A device which cannot be classified as a class I device because the general controls by themselves are insufficient to provide reasonable assurance of the safety and effectiveness of the device, and for which there is sufficient information to establish special controls to provide such assurance, including the promulgation of performance standards, postmarket surveillance, patient registries, development and dissemination of guidelines (including guidelines for the submission of clinical data in premarket notification submissions in accordance with section 360(k) of this title ), recommendations, and other appropriate actions as the Secretary deems necessary to provide such assurance. For a device that is purported or represented to be for a use in supporting or sustaining human life, the Secretary shall examine and identify the special controls, if any, that are necessary to provide adequate assurance of safety and effectiveness and describe how such controls provide such assurance. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text mandated compliance with all applicable laws when using Beacon Platforms. The changed text modifies this, stating that Ginkgo's responsibility to obtain regulatory approvals is waived, and they only need to make 'reasonable efforts' to comply with applicable laws. This directly contradicts the FDA's regulatory authority over medical devices, including diagnostic devices, as described in the Law Explanation and supported by 21 USC 360c, which discusses the classification of devices intended for human use and implies regulatory oversight. The contract is explicitly trying to circumvent FDA approval.
